questions.question: CAIS and CAISI sound alike. How do they actually differ?
options: They are the same organization with two names

CAIS is a non-profit research org publishing risk frameworks and public statements; CAISI is a US-government center within NIST running voluntary lab agreements and unclassified capability evaluations
CAIS is government, CAISI is private
CAISI evaluates models; CAIS builds them
questions.explanation: The acronyms collide; the missions don't overlap — check the URL (nist.gov for CAISI). CAISI is the public, government-facing counterpart to METR's often-NDA-gated lab engagements; reading both gives a fuller picture.

questions.question: What are the four top-level categories in CAIS's widely-cited risk framework?
options: Cost, latency, accuracy, bias
Malicious use, AI races, organizational risks, and rogue AIs
Cyber, bio, chemical, nuclear
Training, deployment, monitoring, retirement
questions.explanation: The categories aren't mutually exclusive — a rogue AI produced by an organization that traded audit for speed in a competitive race is all four at once. It's the most cited societal-scale-risk taxonomy in the literature.

questions.question: Which of the four risk categories is most directly under a practitioner's control, and what are its levers?
options: Rogue AIs; better loss functions
Organizational risk — safety culture (can people escalate without career cost?), rigorous external audits, multi-layered defenses, and information security
AI races; faster shipping
Malicious use; stronger passwords
questions.explanation: Organizational dynamics decide whether the controls of Lessons 10-18 actually ship or are unverified checklist items. CAIS surveys find safety culture — whether concerns can be raised safely — is a strong predictor of the other levers.

questions.question: Why does the lesson highlight California SB-53?
options: It bans all frontier models

If signed it would be the first US state-level catastrophic-risk regulation (capability thresholds, whistleblower protections, incident reporting) — and US state regulation has historically led federal action
It is already federal law
It applies only to chatbots
questions.explanation: Regardless of signing status, the bill's framing shapes how other legislatures approach the problem. Californian practitioners should track its status; others should read it to anticipate the shape of US state-level AI regulation.

questions.question: What is the phase-wide theme that CAIS's framework extends to the societal layer?
options: One regulation can close catastrophic risk
Defense in depth — no single organization, regulation, or framework is sufficient; the ecosystem (labs, external evaluators, government, civil society) only works in combination
Self-regulation by labs is enough
Risk can be fully eliminated with better models
questions.explanation: The running theme of Phase 15 — multi-layered defenses, no single sufficient layer — scales up: lab scaling policies, independent evaluation (METR), government standards (CAISI), and civil-society frameworks (CAIS) are each necessary and none sufficient alone.
